title: The Minor Prophets: Hosea
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: The Minor Prophets - Hosea - Elder Nick Anderson.txt
outline: Context: following the overview of the Minor Prophets
The book of Hosea: background and structure
Hosea's marriage as a living parable of Israel's unfaithfulness
God's covenant love despite Israel's spiritual adultery
Call to return to the Lord
summary: Part of a PM series on the Minor Prophets. Elder Nick Anderson dives into the book of Hosea following the earlier overview of the entire minor prophets. Hosea's dramatic marriage to an unfaithful wife becomes a living parable of Israel's spiritual adultery against the LORD, and yet the book resounds with the covenant faithfulness of God who refuses to abandon His wayward people.
transcript:
Two weeks ago, Pastor Tito started this series on the Minor Prophets and we got a 30,000-foot overview of the whole collection. Tonight we are going to dive into the book of Hosea and we are going to finish it.

## Background on Hosea

We know very little about Hosea outside of what the book itself tells us. It's possible he may have been associated with Beerah of 1 Chronicles 5:6, which would make him from the tribe of Reuben — but that's not certain. His name means *salvation*. The first verse of the book tells us that he prophesied during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah, and Jeroboam II — anywhere from around 753 BC to 722 BC, and possibly further into 715 BC after the fall of the Northern Kingdom. He may have lived in the southern kingdom of Judah after 722 BC.

The theme of the book is God's love — though rejected, it is constant. It is also about God's covenant faithfulness to his people and his zeal for the covenant. The overall purpose of the book is to show that God's love is unfailing despite Israel's unfaithfulness to him.

Hosea was probably a native Israelite. He has detailed knowledge of Israel's geography, seen in chapters 5, 6, 12, and 14. He uses the term *Ephraim* 37 times throughout the book. And in chapter 7:5, he refers to *our king* — referring to the king of Israel. His prophetic call is one of the least dramatic — it seems like God simply spoke to him, and that was it. No grand vision like Isaiah's or Ezekiel's. The word just came to him.

Two interesting things about Hosea: he is the only prophet who actually lived and prophesied in the Northern Kingdom — none of the others did. And Hosea actually *acts out* God's word before he starts to proclaim it. God tells him what to do, he goes and does it, and then he starts to proclaim God's word to the people.

## Hosea's Marriage to Gomer

As was mentioned, Hosea had a very difficult domestic life. He was married to an unfaithful wife named Gomer. This brings us to probably the most difficult interpretive question in the book: was Hosea's marriage a literal historical marriage, or was it allegorical or figurative? And if literal, was Gomer already a prostitute when they married, or did that happen afterward?

The main problem is this: would God — a holy and good God who gave us his law — command one of his prophets to marry a woman who was actually a prostitute? Or, as verse 2 says, *a wife of harlotry with children of harlotry*? This does seem to run contrary to God's explicit command for a priest not to marry a harlot (Leviticus 21:7). And Deuteronomy 22 says a woman proven to be unfaithful is to be put to death. So there's a twofold dilemma: would God lower his standards for a prophet? And would he overlook the standard for purity in a wife?

There are several proposed solutions:

**The hypothetical marriage view** says it wasn't a real relationship — it's just imagery. Hosea starts his ministry and essentially says: *what if I had married a harlot? This is how you are acting, Israel.* I don't prefer this view. I don't like to allegorize the Bible unless the text itself makes that clear.

**The literal view (she was already a harlot)** says Hosea literally married a prostitute, and for the sake of the message, God overlooked the implications.

**The second literal view** says that *harlotry* here doesn't refer to sexual immorality but to idolatry — a usage that does appear elsewhere in Scripture. But this doesn't seem consistent with verse 2, which says the children are also *children of harlotry*.

**The proleptic view** says Gomer was faithful at the beginning of the marriage but would soon become unfaithful — and God is describing her in terms of what she would eventually become. So God tells Hosea: *you are going to marry this woman who will become a harlot after you wed her.*

**The hybrid view** says Gomer was pure at the time of marriage but had leanings in that direction. God revealed to Hosea that she had a heart of harlotry, but he was to marry her anyway. She would be faithful for a short time, and then she would become a prostitute — possibly even a temple prostitute later in their marriage.

These last two are the views I lean toward. The interesting thing about the hybrid view is that this is exactly what Israel did: they were faithful for a little while, and then they left the true worship of God. And God knew they were going to do this — even when he was speaking to Moses during the wilderness wandering, he told them they would be unfaithful. And yet he redeems them, just as he redeems us, even knowing that we are sinful and unfaithful at times.

## Historical Background

The Northern Kingdom around 753 BC had been in a state of prosperity under Jeroboam II, but from roughly 750 to 722 BC it experienced a rapid and catastrophic decline. Over the next 25 years the Northern Kingdom would have six different kings — four of whom were assassinated, two became political prisoners, and only one was actually succeeded by a son.

During this time, the Syro-Ephraimite War saw Israel form an alliance with Syria to hold back the growing Assyrian empire. Pekah of Israel attacked Judah because Judah refused to join the alliance. Judah called on Assyria for help, Assyria attacked Syria, and the result was that much of the Northern Kingdom's territory was subjugated to Assyria — eventually ending in the fall of the Northern Kingdom in 722 BC. As we read through Hosea, you will see references to Egypt and Assyria — the Northern Kingdom was trying to get aid from both, but neither strategy worked, and it ended in their destruction.

The Northern Kingdom had become spiritually very corrupt. The moral decline of the Northern Kingdom is strikingly similar in some ways to what we see in the United States today. Canaanite fertility rituals were being incorporated into Israel's worship — ideas from outside what the Lord had prescribed were being added to their religion. They had sex rites, ritual prostitution — which Gomer was probably involved in — and Baal worship was incorporated into what was ostensibly worship of the Lord. The priests themselves were corrupt, not merely failing to teach the people the true religion, but at times actively sponsoring false religion.

As a point of context, Hosea was prophesying at the same time as Isaiah, Micah, and Amos. Jonah came before this group; then Isaiah, Micah, Amos, and Hosea were all prophesying simultaneously; and Joel would come afterward.

## Outline of Hosea: Chapters 1–3

We're going to work through the book in broad strokes, focusing primarily on chapters 1 through 3.

**Chapter 1:** God instructs Hosea to marry Gomer. He does so. They have their first child. The Lord instructs Hosea to name the child *Jezreel* — because in a little while, God will avenge the bloodshed of Jezreel on the house of Jehu and bring an end to the kingdom of Israel. Jezreel is where Jehu killed all of Ahab's sons and cut off the line of Ahab. Jehu's dynasty started there, and it would end in a similar way. Jehu's line was indeed cut off when Shallum assassinated Zechariah — and this ultimately contributed to the fall of the Northern Kingdom.

They then have a daughter. God says to name her *Lo-Ruhamah*, meaning *no mercy* — for I will no longer have mercy on the house of Israel. Not the kind of name you want to give a child, but that is what the Lord commanded.

Then a third child is born, a son. God says name him *Lo-Ammi* — meaning *not my people* — for you are not my people, and I will not be your God. This is very serious. The Lord is visually and audibly depicting the cutting off of his people.

However — as is very common in the prophets — alongside the announcement of judgment comes a word of eventual restoration. In chapter 2:1, God announces the restoration of Israel, contrasting the names he had given Hosea's children:

> *"Say to your brothers, 'My people,' and to your sisters, 'She has obtained mercy.'"*

**Chapter 2:** God declares he will divorce Israel because of their unfaithfulness, yet in verse 20 comes this remarkable promise:

> *"I will betroth you to me in faithfulness. And you shall know the Lord."*

This is significant because the people didn't think they were estranged from God at all. They were completely clueless about how far they had drifted. They thought they were still serving the true God. And he tells them: when I restore you, *then* you will actually know me. In other words: you don't know me right now, but you will eventually.

**Chapter 3:** Gomer is reunited with Hosea. He purchases her out of slavery for fifteen shekels of silver and a homer and a half of barley — which, if you look back at Exodus 21:32, is roughly the price of a slave. So she had evidently sunk into actual slavery. God instructs Hosea to take her back — possibly fifteen to twenty years after their initial marriage. He does so, takes her home, and then writes the rest of the book.

Verse 5 says:

> *"Afterward the children of Israel shall return and seek the Lord their God, and David their king, and they shall come in fear to the Lord and to his goodness in the latter days."*

Just as Hosea sought out Gomer in slavery, the Lord will seek his people in exile.

## The Three Charges Against Israel (Chapters 4–14)

The third and major section of the book (chapters 4–14) contains three main charges against Israel.

**Charge 1: Failure to acknowledge God (chapters 4–6).** The Lord brings the charge in chapter 4:1:

> *"There is no faithfulness or steadfast love, and no knowledge of God in the land."*

They had divided hearts — wanting to serve God, yet also wanting to serve other gods. And God says: you cannot do that. Chapter 4:6:

> *"My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge; because you have rejected knowledge, I reject you from being a priest to me."*

Chapter 5:4:

> *"Their deeds do not permit them to return to their God, for the spirit of whoredom is within them, and they know not the Lord."*

Yet even in this context there is a call to repentance in chapter 6:1–3:

> *"Come, let us return to the Lord; for he has torn us, that he may heal us; he has struck us down, and he will bind us up. After two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up, that we may live before him. Let us know; let us press on to know the Lord."*

**Charge 2: Breaking God's covenant (chapters 6–11).** Israel was unfaithful. In chapter 8:2, in the midst of all this, Israel still says: *"My God, we know you."* They still don't get it. Their actions betray them. Chapters 9 and 10 deal with their guilt and punishment. Chapter 11 turns a corner and shows God's faithfulness and love toward them.

**Charge 3: Faithlessness (chapters 11–14).**

## Hosea in the New Testament

It's worth noting how much the New Testament uses the book of Hosea.

Romans 9:25 and 1 Peter 2:10 both draw on Hosea's language that those who were *not* God's people will become God's people and those who had *not obtained mercy* will obtain mercy. Some commentators say the New Testament reinterprets these passages to refer only to Gentiles — but a strong case can be made that it refers to both: to Israel, who will be redeemed in their future conversion, *and* to the Gentiles.

Hosea 10:8 is quoted in Luke 23:30 and Revelation 6:16 — the cry that mountains would cover the wicked to hide them from God's judgment.

Hosea 11:1 is quoted in Matthew 2:15: *"Out of Egypt I called my son."* The original refers to Israel coming out of Egypt under Moses. Matthew applies it to Jesus coming out of Egypt as a boy. This requires Israel to be understood as a type of Christ — a case of historical corporate solidarity. It's not an explicit messianic prediction, but the typology is there.

Hosea 13:14 is quoted in 1 Corinthians 15:55:

> *"I will ransom them from the power of the grave; I will redeem them from death. O death, where are your plagues? O grave, where is your destruction?"* (Hosea 13:14)

> *"O death, where is your sting? O Hades, where is your victory? The sting of death is sin, and the strength of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ."* (1 Corinthians 15:55–57)

And Hosea 6:2 — *"After two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up"* — may underlie 1 Corinthians 15:4, which says Christ rose *on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures.*

Hosea is quoted more in the New Testament than you might expect, and the whole book is worthy of our study.

## Closing Question

The question I leave you with is the very question Israel thought they could answer — but couldn't: *do you know the Lord?*

The people of Israel were completely oblivious to the fact that they had no real knowledge of God. And yet they thought they did. This is not a new problem. Jesus addressed it directly in Matthew 7:

> *"Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven. On that day many will say to me, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and cast out demons in your name, and do many mighty works in your name?' And then will I declare to them, 'I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of lawlessness.'"* (Matthew 7:21–23)

The warnings in Scripture to examine ourselves and make sure we are in the faith are for believers — so that we don't fall away. We need to heed them.

I wish I could read chapter 14 to close, because it gives a beautiful picture of what it looks like to repent and of how the Lord restores his people. Hosea is almost thirteen chapters of doom and gloom, with sparks of hope sprinkled throughout — but it ends on a remarkably high note: the people will be brought back into the sheepfold, the Lord will restore Israel, and he will restore us as well if we are repenting.
